protocol_definition.name: Voting
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, then cast private ballots; plurality selects the output.
protocol_definition.visibility: Answers are private before voting; voters see all anonymized proposals during voting.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after ballot aggregation; deterministic lowest-index tie break.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
This plan outlines a 12-week pilot launch of an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students). The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, and academic integrity violations, and escalates to human staff when uncertain. The team includes one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. A baseline plan is provided, followed by a contingency overlay for Weeks 6–8 where one student developer is unavailable and $750 is reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing. The total budget remains at $5,000.

Assumptions:
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting credits or existing infrastructure (e.g., virtual machine, database) at no additional cost beyond the $5,000.
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided in digital format by Week 2.
- No university policies, approvals, or legal guarantees are assumed; all safeguards are technical and procedural.
- Student developers have basic proficiency in Python, NLP libraries, and version control.
- The teaching assistant can handle up to 10 hours/week of escalation and support.
- The faculty sponsor can dedicate 2–3 hours/week for content approval and oversight.
- The pilot runs during a regular academic semester; no major holidays or exam periods conflict with Weeks 6–8.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
Goals:
- Deploy a functional AI course-QA assistant by Week 8 for two courses.
- Achieve ≥80% answer accuracy on a held-out test set of 50 instructor-approved questions by Week 10.
- Ensure <5% of student queries require human escalation by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during the pilot.
- Collect usage data (queries, accuracy, escalation rate, user satisfaction) for post-pilot evaluation.

Non-Goals:
- Not building a general-purpose AI assistant; scope is limited to two courses.
- Not replacing human teaching assistants or instructors.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or scalability.
- Not integrating with university learning management system (LMS) beyond basic API access (if available).
- Not conducting formal user research beyond pilot feedback surveys.

3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map
| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, content owner | Approves course materials, final answer content, escalation thresholds, and pilot continuation after Week 12. |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer (Weeks 1–12) | Technical architecture, code implementation, integration, and testing. |
| Student Developer B | Support developer (Weeks 1–5, then unavailable Weeks 6–8, returns Week 9) | Code contributions, documentation, and testing. |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Support and escalation handler | Reviews escalated queries, provides feedback on answer quality, and collects user feedback. |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide usage data and optional feedback; no decision rights. |
| Project Lead (Planner) | Strategic oversight | Defines milestones, budget allocation, risk management, and decision criteria. |

Decision gates (see Section 4) require faculty sponsor approval.

4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table
| Week | Phase | Activities | Deliverables | Dependencies | Decision Gate |
|------|-------|------------|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | Initiation | Define scope, gather course materials, set up development environment | Project charter, material repository | Faculty sponsor provides materials | Gate 1: Scope approval |
| 2 | Design | Design system architecture, define answer extraction pipeline, plan safeguards | Architecture diagram, safeguard specification | Week 1 deliverables | – |
| 3 | Development (Core) | Build ingestion pipeline for course materials, implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) | Ingestion module, initial RAG prototype | Week 2 design | – |
| 4 | Development (Safeguards) | Implement hallucination detection, privacy filter, academic integrity check, escalation trigger | Safeguard modules | Week 3 prototype | – |
| 5 | Integration & Internal Test | Integrate all modules, run internal tests with 20 sample questions | Integrated system, test report | Week 4 safeguards | Gate 2: Internal test pass (≥70% accuracy) |
| 6 | Pilot Prep | Prepare user interface (simple web chat), create user guide, set up logging | UI, user guide, logging system | Week 5 integration | – |
| 7 | Soft Launch (Course A) | Deploy to Course A (150 students), monitor usage, collect initial feedback | Live system for Course A | Week 6 prep | – |
| 8 | Soft Launch (Course B) | Deploy to Course B (150 students), full pilot live | Live system for both courses | Week 7 soft launch | Gate 3: Full pilot go/no-go |
| 9 | Monitoring & Tuning | Analyze usage data, tune retrieval and safeguards, fix bugs | Tuning report, bug fixes | Week 8 full launch | – |
| 10 | Evaluation | Run formal accuracy test (50 questions), collect user satisfaction survey | Accuracy report, survey results | Week 9 tuning | Gate 4: Evaluation review |
| 11 | Refinement | Implement minor improvements based on evaluation | Updated system | Week 10 evaluation | – |
| 12 | Final Review & Decision | Compile final report, present to faculty sponsor, decide on expand/revise/stop | Final report, decision recommendation | Week 11 refinement | Gate 5: Final decision |

5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation
Resources:
- Faculty sponsor: 2–3 hrs/week (12 weeks)
- Student Developer A: 15 hrs/week (12 weeks)
- Student Developer B: 10 hrs/week (12 weeks)
- TA: 5 hrs/week (12 weeks)

Baseline Budget ($5,000 total):
| Category | Amount | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| Infrastructure (cloud hosting, API credits) | $1,500 | Compute, storage, LLM API calls |
| Privacy review & testing | $1,000 | External privacy consultant (10 hrs @ $100/hr) |
| Student developer stipends | $2,000 | $1,000 each for two developers (flat rate) |
| TA stipend | $500 | Additional compensation for pilot support |
| Contingency reserve | $0 | (None in baseline; all funds allocated) |
| Total | $5,000 | |

6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes
Contingency: Student Developer B unavailable Weeks 6–8. $750 reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing.

Ownership Changes:
- Student Developer A takes over all development tasks during Weeks 6–8, including UI preparation (Week 6) and deployment support (Weeks 7–8).
- TA increases hours from 5 to 10 hrs/week during Weeks 6–8 to assist with testing, user guide creation, and escalation handling.
- Faculty sponsor increases oversight to 4 hrs/week during Weeks 6–8 to review deployment readiness and approve any quick fixes.

Scope Changes:
- Soft launch for Course A (Week 7) remains, but deployment for Course B (Week 8) is delayed by one week (to Week 9) to allow additional testing with reduced developer capacity.
- Full pilot go/no-go gate (Gate 3) moves from Week 8 to Week 9.
- Evaluation (Week 10) shifts to Week 11; final review (Week 12) shifts to Week 13 (but pilot duration remains 12 weeks of active use; final review is delayed by one week).

Dependencies:
- Student Developer A now depends on TA for testing support (previously shared with Developer B).
- Privacy review and testing (now $1,750 total) must be completed by Week 7 (before soft launch) to ensure safeguards are robust.

Testing Changes:
- Additional privacy and hallucination testing is conducted in Weeks 6–7 (using reallocated funds for external consultant).
- Internal test (Week 5) remains unchanged.

Support Changes:
- TA handles more user queries during Weeks 6–8 due to reduced developer availability for bug fixes.
- Escalation process remains the same but TA is primary responder.

Milestones:
- Week 6: Privacy review completed (new milestone).
- Week 7: Soft launch Course A (unchanged).
- Week 9: Soft launch Course B (delayed from Week 8).
- Week 11: Evaluation (delayed from Week 10).
- Week 13: Final review (delayed from Week 12).

Decision Gates:
- Gate 3 (full pilot go/no-go) moves to Week 9.
- Gate 4 (evaluation review) moves to Week 11.
- Gate 5 (final decision) moves to Week 13.

7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation
| Category | Baseline | Reallocation | Revised | Description |
|----------|----------|--------------|---------|-------------|
| Infrastructure | $1,500 | -$750 | $750 | Reduced cloud/API usage; rely on existing credits |
| Privacy review & testing | $1,000 | +$750 | $1,750 | Additional external consultant hours for thorough testing |
| Student developer stipends | $2,000 | $0 | $2,000 | Unchanged (both developers paid full amount) |
| TA stipend | $500 | $0 | $500 | Unchanged |
| Contingency reserve | $0 | $0 | $0 | None |
| Total | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | Budget remains at $5,000 |

8. Risk Register and Mitigations
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Student Developer B unavailable Weeks 6–8 | High (given contingency) | Medium | Developer A takes over; TA increases hours; soft launch delayed for Course B |
| Hallucination or inaccurate answers | Medium | High | Multiple safeguard layers (RAG, confidence threshold, escalation); continuous testing |
| Privacy breach (e.g., student data exposed) | Low | Critical | Privacy filter; no storage of personal data; external privacy review; data minimization |
| Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant provides answers to assignments) | Medium | High | Only answer from approved materials; flag assignment-related queries for human review |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | TA promotes assistant in class; simple UI; quick feedback loop |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Strict reallocation; use existing infrastructure credits; no additional spending |
| Faculty sponsor unavailable | Low | Medium | Pre-approve content and escalation thresholds; TA acts as backup approver |

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
Metrics:
- Answer accuracy: % of correct answers on a held-out test set of 50 questions (target ≥80% by Week 10/11).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human staff (target <5% by Week 12).
- User satisfaction: Average rating on a 1–5 scale from optional survey (target ≥4.0).
- Privacy incidents: Number of confirmed breaches (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: Number of confirmed violations (target 0).
- Adoption rate: % of students who use the assistant at least once (target ≥50% by Week 12).

Data-Collection Plan:
- Log all queries (anonymized) with timestamps, answers, and escalation flags.
- Weekly accuracy spot-checks by TA using 10 random queries.
- Formal accuracy test at Week 10/11 with 50 pre-approved questions.
- Optional user satisfaction survey distributed at Week 10/11.
- Incident log maintained by TA and faculty sponsor.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
Governance:
- Faculty sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any new material added during pilot requires sponsor approval.
- Weekly 30-minute check-in with faculty sponsor, TA, and lead developer.

Content Approval:
- Instructor-approved materials are the sole source for answers.
- No external web search or unapproved documents allowed.
- Answer templates for common question types are pre-approved.

Escalation Process:
- If the assistant’s confidence score is below 0.7, or if the query is flagged as assignment-related, the query is escalated to the TA.
- TA responds within 4 hours during business days.
- If TA cannot answer, escalation to faculty sponsor within 24 hours.
- All escalations are logged for review.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
Expand Criteria (all must be met):
- Answer accuracy ≥85% on final test.
- Escalation rate <3%.
- User satisfaction ≥4.5.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Faculty sponsor recommends expansion to additional courses.

Revise/Extend Criteria (any one met):
- Accuracy between 70% and 84%.
- Escalation rate between 3% and 8%.
- User satisfaction between 3.5 and 4.4.
- Minor privacy or integrity incident (resolved without data exposure).
- Faculty sponsor requests additional features or extended pilot.

Stop Criteria (any one met):
- Accuracy <70%.
- Escalation rate >8%.
- User satisfaction <3.5.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget exceeded or unsustainable.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan as outlined, with the contingency overlay prepared for Weeks 6–8. The reallocation of $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing is a prudent risk mitigation that does not compromise core functionality, as existing university credits can cover reduced infrastructure needs. The delayed soft launch for Course B (Week 9) and shifted decision gates ensure quality is maintained despite reduced developer capacity. The pilot is feasible within the $5,000 budget and team constraints, with clear success metrics and decision criteria for post-pilot evaluation.

1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
This plan outlines a 12-week pilot launch of an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students). The assistant will answer only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, and academic integrity violations, and will escalate to human staff when needed. The team consists of one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. A baseline plan is provided, followed by a contingency where one student developer is unavailable during Weeks 6-8 and $750 is reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing. The total budget remains at $5,000.

Assumptions:
- University IT policies allow deployment of a pilot AI tool with restricted access (e.g., university login).
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided in digital format (PDF, text files) by Week 2.
- The two undergraduate courses have stable enrollment of ~150 students each.
- The part-time faculty sponsor can dedicate 2-3 hours per week; the teaching assistant can dedicate 5 hours per week; each student developer can dedicate 10 hours per week.
- No existing AI infrastructure is available; the team will use a cloud-based LLM API (e.g., OpenAI) with a pay-per-use model.
- Privacy review and testing can be conducted by the faculty sponsor and teaching assistant with external consultation if needed.
- No university policies, approvals, completed results, or legal guarantees are assumed beyond standard academic conduct.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
Goals:
- Deploy a functional AI course-QA assistant by Week 8 for two courses.
- Ensure the assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials (no external knowledge).
- Implement safeguards: hallucination detection (confidence threshold), privacy (no storage of student personal data), academic integrity (no direct answers to graded assignments), and escalation to human staff (teaching assistant or faculty) for ambiguous or off-topic queries.
- Collect usage data (number of queries, topics, escalation rates) for evaluation.
- Stay within $5,000 budget.

Non-Goals:
- Not to replace human teaching assistants or faculty office hours.
- Not to provide answers for graded assignments or exams.
- Not to scale beyond the two pilot courses during the 12-week period.
- Not to achieve perfect accuracy; the pilot is for learning and improvement.

3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map
| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor (part-time) | Project owner, content approver | Approves course materials, final content for assistant, escalation protocol, and go/no-go decisions. |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer (baseline) | Technical architecture, coding, integration, testing. |
| Student Developer B | Support developer (baseline) | Coding, documentation, testing, bug fixes. |
| Teaching Assistant | QA and support lead | Defines test cases, conducts user testing, handles escalations, collects feedback. |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide usage data and feedback (optional). |
| Project (no external oversight) | N/A | All decisions made by faculty sponsor in consultation with team. |

4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table
| Week | Milestone | Activities | Dependencies | Testing | Support | Decision Gate |
|------|-----------|------------|--------------|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Project kickoff | Define scope, roles, tech stack (e.g., RAG pipeline with LLM API). | None | None | None | Approve tech stack and timeline. |
| 2 | Content ingestion | Faculty sponsor provides approved materials; developers build ingestion pipeline. | Week 1 decision | Unit test ingestion | None | None |
| 3 | Core assistant build | Implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with approved materials. | Week 2 content | Integration test | None | None |
| 4 | Safeguard implementation | Add hallucination detection (confidence threshold), privacy filter (no PII storage), academic integrity filter (block graded assignment queries), escalation trigger. | Week 3 core | Unit test safeguards | None | None |
| 5 | Internal testing | Team tests with sample queries; teaching assistant creates test cases. | Week 4 safeguards | Full system test | TA handles test feedback | Go/no-go for pilot launch. |
| 6 | Pilot launch (Course A) | Deploy to Course A (~150 students); monitor usage. | Week 5 go | Live monitoring | TA handles escalations | None |
| 7 | Pilot expansion (Course B) | Deploy to Course B; collect feedback. | Week 6 | A/B test (if needed) | TA + faculty handle escalations | None |
| 8 | Mid-pilot review | Analyze usage data, fix bugs, adjust safeguards. | Weeks 6-7 data | Regression test | TA collects feedback | Decide on continuation. |
| 9 | Optimization | Improve response accuracy, reduce false escalations. | Week 8 review | Performance test | TA monitors | None |
| 10 | User survey | Distribute survey to students; collect qualitative feedback. | Week 9 | None | TA administers survey | None |
| 11 | Final analysis | Compile data, draft report. | Week 10 survey | None | Faculty reviews report | None |
| 12 | Project close | Present findings, decide on future. | Week 11 report | None | None | Expand/Revise/Stop decision. |

5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation
| Resource | Allocation | Cost |
|----------|------------|------|
| Faculty Sponsor | 2-3 hrs/week (unpaid) | $0 |
| Student Developer A | 10 hrs/week (unpaid, course credit) | $0 |
| Student Developer B | 10 hrs/week (unpaid, course credit) | $0 |
| Teaching Assistant | 5 hrs/week (stipend) | $1,000 (estimated stipend for 12 weeks) |
| Cloud LLM API (e.g., OpenAI) | Pay-per-use for 300 students, 12 weeks | $1,500 |
| Infrastructure (hosting, database, vector store) | Cloud hosting (e.g., AWS, Pinecone) | $1,500 |
| Privacy review and testing | External consultant or tool (e.g., privacy audit) | $500 |
| Contingency | Unallocated | $500 |
| **Total** | | **$5,000** |

6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes
Contingency: At the end of Week 5, Student Developer B becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8. Exactly $750 originally allocated to infrastructure ($1,500) is reallocated to privacy review and testing ($500 baseline + $750 = $1,250). The total budget remains $5,000.

Scope changes:
- During Weeks 6-8, only Course A will be piloted (Course B pilot delayed to Week 9). This reduces scope but maintains focus.
- Privacy review and testing are expanded: additional testing for hallucination, privacy, and academic integrity safeguards, plus a formal privacy review by the faculty sponsor and teaching assistant (using the reallocated funds for external consultation if needed).

Ownership changes:
- Student Developer A takes over all development tasks (coding, bug fixes, monitoring) during Weeks 6-8.
- Teaching Assistant increases hours to 8 hrs/week (from 5) to handle additional testing and support (funded by reallocated $750? No, TA stipend is separate; the $750 is for privacy review/testing, not TA hours. TA hours are unpaid beyond stipend; faculty sponsor can adjust TA workload).
- Faculty Sponsor increases oversight to 4 hrs/week during Weeks 6-8 to assist with testing and decision-making.

Dependencies:
- Week 6-8: Course B pilot depends on Student Developer B’s return in Week 9. If B does not return, Course B pilot may be canceled or delayed further.
- Privacy review and testing (now $1,250) must be completed by Week 8 to inform mid-pilot review.

Testing changes:
- Expanded privacy review: test for PII leakage, data retention, and compliance with university guidelines (assumed).
- Additional safeguard testing: simulate adversarial queries (e.g., attempts to get graded answers, off-topic questions).

Support changes:
- Teaching Assistant handles all escalations during Weeks 6-8 (no backup from Student Developer B).
- Faculty Sponsor is on-call for complex escalations.

Milestones changes:
- Week 6: Pilot launch for Course A only (not both).
- Week 7: No Course B launch; instead, conduct expanded privacy review and testing.
- Week 8: Mid-pilot review includes results of expanded testing; decide on Course B launch in Week 9.

Decision gates changes:
- Week 5 go/no-go remains, but now includes contingency plan approval.
- Week 8 decision gate: decide whether to launch Course B in Week 9 (if Student Developer B returns) or cancel Course B pilot.

7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation
| Resource | Baseline Cost | Revised Cost | Change |
|----------|---------------|--------------|--------|
| Faculty Sponsor | $0 | $0 | $0 |
| Student Developer A | $0 | $0 | $0 |
| Student Developer B | $0 | $0 | $0 |
| Teaching Assistant | $1,000 | $1,000 | $0 |
| Cloud LLM API | $1,500 | $1,500 | $0 |
| Infrastructure (hosting, database, vector store) | $1,500 | $750 | -$750 |
| Privacy review and testing | $500 | $1,250 | +$750 |
| Contingency | $500 | $500 | $0 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **$0** |

Note: Infrastructure is reduced to $750 (e.g., use a cheaper vector store or reduce hosting capacity for one course instead of two). Privacy review and testing increased to $1,250 (e.g., external consultant for privacy audit, additional testing tools).

8. Risk Register and Mitigations
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Student developer unavailable (Weeks 6-8) | High | High | Contingency plan: reduce scope to one course, reallocate budget, increase TA and faculty hours. |
| Hallucination (incorrect answers) | Medium | High | Implement confidence threshold; escalate low-confidence responses to human. |
| Privacy breach (PII leakage) | Low | Very High | Privacy filter; no storage of student personal data; regular audits. |
| Academic integrity violation (answers to graded work) | Medium | High | Filter queries for assignment keywords; escalate to faculty. |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Use pay-per-use API; monitor usage weekly; contingency fund. |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | Promote via course announcements; TA encourages use. |
| Technical failure (e.g., API downtime) | Low | Medium | Use fallback: manual FAQ page; escalate to TA. |

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
Metrics:
- Usage: Number of queries per week, unique users, topics.
- Accuracy: Percentage of queries answered correctly (sampled by TA).
- Escalation rate: Percentage of queries escalated to human.
- Hallucination rate: Percentage of answers with incorrect information (sampled).
- Privacy incidents: Number of PII leaks (target: 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: Number of attempts to get graded answers (target: 0).
- User satisfaction: Survey score (1-5) at Week 10.
- Budget adherence: Total spend vs. $5,000.

Data-collection plan:
- Log all queries (anonymized) with timestamps, topics, and responses.
- TA manually reviews a random 10% of queries weekly for accuracy and hallucination.
- Track escalation events and reasons.
- Survey students in Week 10 (optional, anonymous).
- Monitor API usage and costs weekly.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
Governance:
- Faculty Sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any changes to the assistant’s knowledge base require faculty approval.
- Weekly team check-ins (15 minutes) to review progress and issues.

Content approval:
- Only instructor-approved materials (syllabus, lecture notes, readings) are ingested.
- Faculty Sponsor reviews and signs off on the final content list by Week 2.
- No external sources (e.g., web search) are allowed.

Escalation process:
- If the assistant cannot answer with high confidence (below threshold), it responds: “I cannot answer this with confidence. Please contact your TA or instructor.”
- If a query appears to be a graded assignment, the assistant responds: “I cannot help with graded assignments. Please contact your instructor.”
- All escalations are logged and forwarded to the Teaching Assistant, who responds within 24 hours.
- If the TA cannot resolve, the Faculty Sponsor is notified.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
Expand criteria (all must be met):
- Usage: At least 50% of students (150 out of 300) have used the assistant at least once.
- Accuracy: At least 90% of sampled queries are answered correctly.
- Escalation rate: Less than 10% of queries are escalated.
- Hallucination rate: Less than 2% of sampled answers contain incorrect information.
- Privacy incidents: Zero.
- Academic integrity incidents: Zero.
- User satisfaction: Average survey score of 4.0 or higher (out of 5).
- Budget: Under $5,000.
- Decision: Propose expansion to additional courses or full deployment.

Revise/Extend criteria (if some metrics are met but not all):
- Usage: At least 30% of students have used the assistant.
- Accuracy: At least 80% of sampled queries are answered correctly.
- Escalation rate: Less than 20%.
- Hallucination rate: Less than 5%.
- Privacy and integrity incidents: Zero.
- User satisfaction: Average survey score of 3.0 or higher.
- Budget: Under $5,000.
- Decision: Revise the assistant (e.g., improve content, safeguards, or user interface) and extend the pilot for another 4-8 weeks.

Stop criteria (if any of the following occur):
- Privacy breach (any PII leak).
- Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant provides answers to graded assignments).
- Hallucination rate above 10% after mitigation attempts.
- Budget overrun beyond $5,000.
- User satisfaction below 2.0.
- Decision: Shut down the pilot and do not deploy further.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan as described, with the contingency plan ready for implementation at the end of Week 5 if Student Developer B becomes unavailable. The contingency reduces scope to one course during Weeks 6-8 but maintains core functionality and expands privacy review and testing. The budget remains within $5,000. The pilot is feasible given the team size and resources, and the decision criteria provide clear guidance for future steps.

**1. Executive Summary and Assumptions**

This plan outlines a 12-week pilot launch of an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students). The assistant will answer only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and human escalation. The team includes one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. A baseline plan is provided, followed by a contingency overlay where one student developer is unavailable during Weeks 6-8 and $750 is reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing. **Assumptions:**
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting credits or existing infrastructure (e.g., a virtual machine) that can be used at minimal cost.
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided in digital format (PDFs, lecture notes, syllabi) by Week 1.
- The two student developers have basic proficiency in Python, natural language processing libraries, and version control.
- The teaching assistant (TA) has 5 hours/week available for testing and support.
- The faculty sponsor has 2 hours/week for oversight and content approval.
- No existing university AI policy prohibits this pilot; no specific approvals are assumed beyond standard course-level permissions.
- The pilot runs during a regular academic semester; Weeks 1-12 align with the semester calendar.
- The assistant is deployed as a web-based chatbot (e.g., using a lightweight framework like Streamlit or Gradio) with a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) backend using open-source models (e.g., sentence transformers, a small LLM via API with no additional cost, or a local model).
- The $5,000 budget covers any API costs, cloud compute, privacy review tools, and testing services; labor is unpaid (volunteer/student project).

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**

**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI course-QA assistant by Week 8 that answers questions from instructor-approved materials for two courses.
- Achieve ≥80% accuracy on a curated test set of 50 course-specific questions by Week 10.
- Ensure zero privacy breaches (no student data stored or shared) and zero hallucinated answers from approved materials (answers must cite source).
- Provide a clear escalation path to human staff (TA/faculty) for unanswered or sensitive questions.
- Collect usage data (questions asked, response accuracy, escalation rate) to inform a decision on expansion.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not intended to replace human instruction or office hours.
- Not designed for grading, assessment, or personalized tutoring.
- Not a production-grade system; no uptime guarantees.
- No integration with university learning management system (LMS) beyond manual material upload.
- No support for courses beyond the two pilot courses.

| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|

| Student Developer A | Lead developer (Weeks 1-12) | Technical design, implementation, and code review; primary decision-maker on architecture. |
| Student Developer B | Co-developer (Weeks 1-5, then unavailable Weeks 6-8, returns Week 9) | Implementation, testing, and documentation; shares technical decisions with Developer A. |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Testing and support | Defines test cases, conducts user acceptance testing, handles student escalations, provides feedback. |
| Analyst (you) | Strategic planner | Proposes plan, monitors milestones, recommends Expand/Revise/Stop after Week 12. |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide feedback via optional surveys; no decision rights. |

**Decision Gates:**
- **Gate 1 (Week 4):** Faculty sponsor approves prototype demo with 10 sample Q&A pairs. If not approved, project stops.
- **Gate 2 (Week 8):** TA confirms system passes 80% accuracy on test set. If not, revise or stop.
- **Gate 3 (Week 12):** Analyst recommends Expand, Revise/Extend, or Stop based on success metrics.

**4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table**

| Week | Milestone | Activities | Dependencies | Testing | Support | Ownership |
|------|-----------|------------|--------------|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Project kickoff | Define scope, gather materials, set up repo | Faculty provides materials | None | None | Faculty, both developers |
| 2 | Data preparation | Chunk and embed course materials | Week 1 materials | Verify chunking accuracy | TA reviews sample chunks | Developer A, Developer B |
| 3 | Prototype RAG pipeline | Build retrieval and generation (local model) | Week 2 embeddings | Unit test retrieval | TA tests 5 queries | Developer A (lead), Developer B |
| 4 | Gate 1: Prototype demo | Demo 10 Q&A to faculty | Week 3 pipeline | Faculty approval | Faculty reviews | Faculty (decision), both developers |
| 5 | Safeguard implementation | Add hallucination detection, privacy filter, escalation button | Week 4 approval | Test safeguards with adversarial inputs | TA creates test cases | Developer B (lead), Developer A |
| 6 | Integration and UI | Build web interface, connect to backend | Week 5 safeguards | UI/UX testing | TA tests 20 queries | Developer A (lead), Developer B |
| 7 | Internal testing | Full system test with 50 questions | Week 6 UI | Accuracy and escalation tests | TA runs test suite | Developer A, TA |
| 8 | Gate 2: Accuracy review | Achieve ≥80% accuracy; faculty sign-off | Week 7 test results | Final test set | Faculty reviews | Faculty (decision), TA, Developer A |
| 9 | Soft launch to 50 students | Limited deployment for feedback | Week 8 approval | Monitor logs, collect feedback | TA handles escalations | Developer A, TA |
| 10 | Iterate based on feedback | Fix bugs, improve responses | Week 9 feedback | Regression testing | TA monitors | Developer A, Developer B (returns) |
| 11 | Full launch to 300 students | Open to all students | Week 10 iteration | Monitor system load | TA and faculty on call | Developer A, TA, faculty |
| 12 | Data collection and analysis | Collect metrics, survey students | Week 11 data | Final accuracy check | Analyst compiles report | Analyst, TA |

**5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation**

**Resources:**
- Faculty sponsor: 2 hrs/week (24 hrs total)
- Developer A: 10 hrs/week (120 hrs total)
- Developer B: 10 hrs/week (120 hrs total)
- TA: 5 hrs/week (60 hrs total)

**Baseline Budget ($5,000):**
- Cloud compute (API calls, hosting): $2,000
- Privacy review tools (e.g., static analysis, data anonymization software): $500
- Testing services (e.g., user testing platform, survey tool): $500
- Contingency reserve: $1,000
- Miscellaneous (e.g., documentation, domain name): $1,000

**6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes**

**Contingency Event:** Developer B becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8. $750 originally allocated to infrastructure (cloud compute) is reallocated to privacy review and testing.

**Scope Changes:**
- Week 6-8: Developer A takes full ownership of integration, UI, and internal testing. TA increases testing hours from 5 to 8 hrs/week (compensated from reallocated budget for TA stipend or tools). Faculty sponsor increases oversight to 3 hrs/week.
- Week 6: UI development is simplified (fewer features, basic interface). Privacy review is accelerated using reallocated funds for automated privacy scanning tools.
- Week 7: Testing is more focused on critical paths (accuracy, escalation, privacy). Some non-critical edge cases are deferred to Week 9.
- Week 8: Gate 2 decision is based on a reduced test set (40 questions instead of 50) to accommodate the delay.

**Dependency Changes:**
- Week 6-8 tasks that depended on Developer B (e.g., UI design, safeguard testing) now depend solely on Developer A and TA.
- The soft launch (Week 9) is delayed to Week 10 if Gate 2 is not met by Week 8.

**Ownership Changes:**
- Developer A: Sole technical lead for Weeks 6-8; all code and testing decisions.
- TA: Elevated to co-testing lead; responsible for creating and executing test cases with Developer A.
- Faculty sponsor: Approves any scope reductions (e.g., simplified UI) and reviews privacy report.

**Testing Changes:**
- Week 7: TA runs a reduced test suite (40 questions) with emphasis on hallucination and privacy safeguards.
- Week 8: Accuracy threshold remains ≥80% but on the reduced set.

**Support Changes:**
- During Weeks 6-8, TA handles all student queries (none yet, but preparation). Faculty sponsor is primary escalation point for technical issues if Developer A is overwhelmed.

**Milestone Changes:**
- Week 8 Gate 2: Decision uses reduced test set. If not met, a one-week extension is considered (but not assumed; plan for Week 9 as revised Gate 2).

**Decision Gate Changes:**
- Gate 2 (Week 8): Faculty sponsor may approve with condition that remaining features are added in Week 9.

**7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation**

| Category | Baseline | Change | Revised |
|----------|----------|--------|---------|
| Cloud compute (infrastructure) | $2,000 | -$750 | $1,250 |
| Privacy review tools | $500 | +$500 | $1,000 |
| Testing services | $500 | +$250 | $750 |
| Contingency reserve | $1,000 | $0 | $1,000 |
| Miscellaneous | $1,000 | $0 | $1,000 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$0** | **$5,000** |

**Explanation:** $750 is moved from cloud compute to privacy review ($500) and testing ($250). Cloud compute is reduced by using a smaller instance or fewer API calls (e.g., batch processing at off-peak times). Privacy review tools include automated scanning for data leakage. Testing services cover additional TA hours or a paid testing platform.

**8. Risk Register and Mitigations**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Developer B unavailable (Weeks 6-8) | High | Medium | Developer A takes lead; TA increases hours; scope simplified. |
| Hallucination from AI | Medium | High | Implement retrieval-only answers with source citation; use confidence threshold; human escalation for low-confidence. |
| Privacy breach (student data) | Low | Critical | No storage of student data; use anonymized logs; privacy review in Week 6. |
| Low accuracy on test set | Medium | High | Iterative testing; faculty review of materials; fallback to human-only support. |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Strict reallocation; use free tiers where possible. |
| Faculty sponsor unavailable | Low | Medium | Pre-approve materials and escalation protocol; TA as backup approver. |
| Student misuse (e.g., cheating) | Medium | Medium | Include academic integrity warning; log all queries; escalate suspicious patterns to faculty. |

**9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan**

**Metrics (measured after Week 12):**
- **Accuracy:** Percentage of correct answers on a 50-question test set (target ≥80%).
- **Escalation rate:** Percentage of queries escalated to human staff (target <20%).
- **User satisfaction:** Average rating from student survey (target ≥3.5/5).
- **Privacy incidents:** Number of data breaches (target 0).
- **Usage:** Number of unique users and queries (target ≥100 users).
- **Response time:** Average time to answer (target <5 seconds).

**Data-Collection Plan:**
- Log all queries (anonymized) with timestamps, response, and escalation flag.
- Weekly accuracy checks by TA using a random sample of 10 queries.
- End-of-pilot survey to all students (optional, anonymous).
- Faculty sponsor reviews logs for academic integrity concerns.

**10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process**

**Governance:**
- Weekly 30-minute check-in with faculty sponsor, Developer A, and TA.
- Decision gates at Weeks 4, 8, and 12.
- All code changes reviewed by Developer A (or Developer B when available).

**Content Approval:**
- Faculty sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any new material added during the pilot must be approved within 24 hours.
- The assistant may only answer from approved materials; any deviation triggers an escalation.

**Escalation Process:**
- If the assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or detects a sensitive topic (e.g., grades, personal info), it displays: "I cannot answer this. Please contact your TA or instructor."
- The query is logged and sent to the TA via email.
- TA responds within 24 hours (during business days).
- If TA cannot answer, faculty sponsor is notified.
- For privacy or academic integrity concerns, faculty sponsor decides on further action.

**11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)**

**Expand Criteria (all must be met):**
- Accuracy ≥80% on final test set.
- Escalation rate <20%.
- User satisfaction ≥3.5/5.
- Zero privacy incidents.
- At least 100 unique users.
- Faculty sponsor recommends expansion to additional courses.

**Revise/Extend Criteria (any one met):**
- Accuracy between 60-79%.
- Escalation rate 20-30%.
- User satisfaction 2.5-3.4/5.
- Minor privacy incident (e.g., log exposure) resolved.
- Faculty sponsor requests changes before expansion.

**Stop Criteria (any one met):**
- Accuracy <60%.
- Escalation rate >30%.
- User satisfaction <2.5/5.
- Any privacy breach involving student data.
- Faculty sponsor vetoes continuation.
- Budget exceeded without justification.

**12. Final Recommendation**

Proceed with the baseline plan as outlined. The contingency overlay for Weeks 6-8 (Developer B unavailable, $750 reallocation) is manageable with scope simplification and increased TA involvement. The plan includes clear decision gates, risk mitigations, and measurable success criteria. The pilot is feasible within the $5,000 budget and team constraints. Recommend approval by faculty sponsor at Week 1 kickoff.

intermediate_messages.content:
**1. Executive Summary and Assumptions**

This plan outlines a 12-week pilot launch of an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students). The assistant will answer only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team consists of one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant (TA), and a $5,000 budget. The baseline plan assumes full team availability. A contingency at Week 5 requires one student developer to be unavailable during Weeks 6-8 and $750 originally for infrastructure to be reallocated to privacy review and testing. The plan shows how scope, ownership, dependencies, testing, support, milestones, and decision gates change under this contingency.

**Assumptions:**
- University has no pre-existing AI policy; this pilot will inform future policy.
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided in digital format by Week 2.
- Student developers have basic AI/LLM experience; TA has course content expertise.
- Faculty sponsor can dedicate ~2 hours/week; TA ~5 hours/week; developers ~10 hours/week each.
- $5,000 budget is for cloud services, API costs, and testing tools; no salaries included.
- Privacy review is conducted by a university privacy office (no cost assumed for their time).
- No legal guarantees or completed results are assumed; all approvals are hypothetical.

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**

**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant that answers course-related questions using only instructor-approved materials.
- Achieve <5% hallucination rate on a test set of 100 questions by Week 10.
- Ensure 100% of student queries are answered within 24 hours (AI or human escalation).
- Collect usage data to inform a full-scale rollout decision.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose AI tutor.
- Not replacing human TA support.
- Not achieving perfect accuracy; escalation is a feature.
- Not integrating with university LMS (manual upload of materials).

**3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map**

| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Project owner, content approval | Approves course materials, final sign-off on deployment, stop/go decisions |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer, architecture | Technical design, code implementation, testing |
| Student Developer B | Developer, integration | API integration, data pipeline, documentation |
| Teaching Assistant | Content QA, user support | Validates answers, handles escalations, collects student feedback |
| Privacy Office (external) | Reviewer | Approves privacy safeguards (no cost assumed) |
| Students | End users | Provide feedback via surveys (opt-in) |

**Decision Gates:** Faculty sponsor must approve at Weeks 4, 8, and 12.

**4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table**

| Week | Milestone | Owner | Dependencies | Testing | Support |
|------|-----------|-------|--------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Project kickoff, define scope | Faculty sponsor | None | None | None |
| 2 | Collect and digitize course materials | TA | Faculty approval | None | None |
| 3 | Build prototype AI pipeline (retrieval + LLM) | Dev A | Materials from Week 2 | Unit tests on 10 questions | Dev B assists |
| 4 | Integrate safeguards (hallucination filter, privacy check, academic integrity flag) | Dev A, Dev B | Prototype from Week 3 | Integration tests | Faculty review |
| 5 | Internal testing with TA (50 questions) | TA, Dev A | Safeguards from Week 4 | Accuracy, latency, escalation tests | Dev B on standby |
| 6 | Beta launch to 50 students | Faculty sponsor | Internal test pass | User acceptance testing (UAT) | TA handles escalations |
| 7 | Collect feedback, iterate on safeguards | Dev A, Dev B | Beta feedback | A/B test on fixes | TA monitors |
| 8 | Full launch to 300 students | Faculty sponsor | Iteration from Week 7 | Load testing | TA + Dev A on call |
| 9 | Monitor and patch | Dev A, Dev B | Full launch | Continuous monitoring | TA escalates |
| 10 | Mid-pilot review, collect metrics | Faculty sponsor | Data from Weeks 6-9 | Survey analysis | All |
| 11 | Final adjustments | Dev A, Dev B | Review findings | Regression tests | TA |
| 12 | Pilot close, report, decision criteria | Faculty sponsor | All data | Final validation | All |

**5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation**

| Resource | Allocation | Cost |
|----------|------------|------|
| Cloud compute (API calls, hosting) | Weeks 1-12 | $2,000 |
| LLM API credits | Weeks 3-12 | $1,500 |
| Testing tools (automated QA) | Weeks 4-10 | $500 |
| Privacy review (external) | Week 4 | $500 |
| Contingency reserve | - | $500 |
| **Total** | | **$5,000** |

**6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes**

**Contingency Trigger:** At end of Week 5, one student developer (Dev B) becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8.

**Changes:**
- **Scope:** Beta launch (Week 6) is limited to 30 students (instead of 50). Full launch (Week 8) is delayed to Week 9. No new features added during Weeks 6-8; only critical bug fixes.
- **Ownership:** Dev A takes over all development and integration tasks. TA assumes additional documentation and testing duties. Faculty sponsor increases oversight to 3 hours/week during this period.
- **Dependencies:** Week 6 beta launch depends on Dev A completing safeguard hardening by end of Week 5 (no change). Week 7 iteration is replaced by a "stabilization week" with no new features.
- **Testing:** UAT is reduced to 20 students (from 50). Load testing is postponed to Week 8 (after Dev B returns).
- **Support:** TA handles all escalations; Dev A is on call for technical issues only during business hours.
- **Milestones:** Week 6 milestone becomes "Limited beta launch (30 students)." Week 7 milestone becomes "Stabilization and bug fixes." Week 8 milestone becomes "Privacy review completion and load test preparation."
- **Decision Gates:** Faculty sponsor holds an emergency gate at end of Week 5 to approve contingency plan. Next gate moves from Week 8 to Week 9 (post full launch).

**7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation**

| Original Item | Original Cost | Revised Cost | Change |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|--------|
| Cloud compute | $2,000 | $1,500 | -$500 |
| LLM API credits | $1,500 | $1,250 | -$250 |
| Testing tools | $500 | $500 | No change |
| Privacy review (external) | $500 | $1,000 | +$500 |
| Additional privacy testing (internal) | $0 | $250 | +$250 |
| Contingency reserve | $500 | $500 | No change |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **$0 net** |

**Explanation:** $750 originally allocated to infrastructure (cloud compute $500 + API credits $250) is reallocated to privacy review ($500 more for external review) and additional privacy testing ($250 for internal tools/scripts). Total budget remains $5,000.

**8. Risk Register and Mitigations**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Developer unavailability (realized) | High | High | Cross-train TA on basic dev tasks; reduce scope; increase faculty oversight |
| Hallucination rate >5% | Medium | High | Implement confidence threshold; escalate uncertain answers to TA |
| Privacy breach | Low | Critical | Reallocate budget to privacy review; anonymize all queries; no storage of PII |
| Academic integrity violation (students using assistant to cheat) | Medium | Medium | Flag questions asking for direct answers; redirect to study resources; log all queries |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | Incentivize with extra credit (faculty decision); send weekly reminders |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Use contingency reserve; cap API usage per student per day |

**9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan**

| Metric | Target | Data Source | Collection Method |
|--------|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Hallucination rate | <5% | Test set of 100 questions | Manual review by TA weekly |
| Response time | <5 seconds | System logs | Automated monitoring |
| Escalation rate | <20% of queries | Logs | Count of human handoffs |
| Student satisfaction | >3.5/5 | Survey | Week 10 and Week 12 surveys |
| Query volume | >500 total | Logs | Automated count |
| Privacy incidents | 0 | Incident log | Manual tracking |

**10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process**

- **Content Approval:** Faculty sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion. Any updates require re-approval.
- **Safeguards:** Hallucination filter (confidence <0.7 triggers escalation), privacy filter (redacts PII), academic integrity flag (detects "write my essay" patterns).
- **Escalation:** If AI cannot answer confidently or query violates policy, it is routed to TA within 1 hour. TA responds within 24 hours. If TA cannot answer, escalate to faculty sponsor.
- **Audit Trail:** All queries and responses logged (anonymized) for review.
- **Weekly Review:** TA and faculty sponsor review escalation logs and accuracy samples.

**11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)**

**Expand Criteria (all must be met):**
- Hallucination rate <3% on final test set.
- Student satisfaction >4.0/5.
- Escalation rate <15%.
- Zero privacy incidents.
- Faculty sponsor approves expansion to additional courses.
- Budget for full-scale deployment secured (separate from pilot).

**Revise/Extend Criteria (any one met):**
- Hallucination rate between 3% and 5%.
- Student satisfaction between 3.0 and 4.0.
- Escalation rate between 15% and 25%.
- Minor privacy incident (e.g., one PII exposure, corrected within 24 hours).
- Extend pilot by 4 weeks to gather more data.

**Stop Criteria (any one met):**
- Hallucination rate >5% after all mitigations.
- Student satisfaction <3.0.
- Major privacy incident (e.g., data breach).
- Budget overrun >10% without contingency.
- Faculty sponsor withdraws support.

**12. Final Recommendation**

Proceed with the baseline plan, but prepare the contingency overlay (reduced scope, ownership changes, reallocated budget) for immediate activation if a developer becomes unavailable. The contingency is designed to maintain core functionality while protecting privacy and academic integrity. The $750 reallocation from infrastructure to privacy review is a prudent trade-off given the sensitivity of student data. The decision criteria after Week 12 provide clear, measurable thresholds for next steps. This plan is conservative, risk-aware, and aligned with the pilot's constraints.

The team includes one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. A baseline plan is provided, followed by a contingency overlay for Weeks 6–8 where one student developer is unavailable and $750 is reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing. The total budget remains at $5,000.

Assumptions:
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting credits or existing infrastructure (e.g., virtual machine, database) at no additional cost beyond the $5,000.
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided in digital format by Week 2.
- No university policies, approvals, or legal guarantees are assumed; all safeguards are technical and procedural.
- Student developers have basic proficiency in Python, NLP libraries, and version control.
- The teaching assistant can handle up to 10 hours/week of escalation and support.
- The faculty sponsor can dedicate 2–3 hours/week for content approval and oversight.
- The pilot runs during a regular academic semester; no major holidays or exam periods conflict with Weeks 6–8.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
Goals:
- Deploy a functional AI course-QA assistant by Week 8 for two courses.
- Achieve ≥80% answer accuracy on a held-out test set of 50 instructor-approved questions by Week 10.
- Ensure <5% of student queries require human escalation by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during the pilot.
- Collect usage data (queries, accuracy, escalation rate, user satisfaction) for post-pilot evaluation.

Non-Goals:
- Not building a general-purpose AI assistant; scope is limited to two courses.
- Not replacing human teaching assistants or instructors.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or scalability.
- Not integrating with university learning management system (LMS) beyond basic API access (if available).
- Not conducting formal user research beyond pilot feedback surveys.

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
Metrics:
- Answer accuracy: % of correct answers on a held-out test set of 50 questions (target ≥80% by Week 10/11).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human staff (target <5% by Week 12).
- User satisfaction: Average rating on a 1–5 scale from optional survey (target ≥4.0).
- Privacy incidents: Number of confirmed breaches (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: Number of confirmed violations (target 0).
- Adoption rate: % of students who use the assistant at least once (target ≥50% by Week 12).

Data-Collection Plan:
- Log all queries (anonymized) with timestamps, answers, and escalation flags.
- Weekly accuracy spot-checks by TA using 10 random queries.
- Formal accuracy test at Week 10/11 with 50 pre-approved questions.
- Optional user satisfaction survey distributed at Week 10/11.
- Incident log maintained by TA and faculty sponsor.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
Governance:
- Faculty sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any new material added during pilot requires sponsor approval.
- Weekly 30-minute check-in with faculty sponsor, TA, and lead developer.

Content Approval:
- Instructor-approved materials are the sole source for answers.
- No external web search or unapproved documents allowed.
- Answer templates for common question types are pre-approved.

Escalation Process:
- If the assistant’s confidence score is below 0.7, or if the query is flagged as assignment-related, the query is escalated to the TA.
- TA responds within 4 hours during business days.
- If TA cannot answer, escalation to faculty sponsor within 24 hours.
- All escalations are logged for review.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
Expand Criteria (all must be met):
- Answer accuracy ≥85% on final test.
- Escalation rate <3%.
- User satisfaction ≥4.5.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Faculty sponsor recommends expansion to additional courses.

Revise/Extend Criteria (any one met):
- Accuracy between 70% and 84%.
- Escalation rate between 3% and 8%.
- User satisfaction between 3.5 and 4.4.
- Minor privacy or integrity incident (resolved without data exposure).
- Faculty sponsor requests additional features or extended pilot.

Stop Criteria (any one met):
- Accuracy <70%.
- Escalation rate >8%.
- User satisfaction <3.5.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget exceeded or unsustainable.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan as outlined, with the contingency overlay prepared for Weeks 6–8. The reallocation of $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing is a prudent risk mitigation that does not compromise core functionality, as existing university credits can cover reduced infrastructure needs. The delayed soft launch for Course B (Week 9) and shifted decision gates ensure quality is maintained despite reduced developer capacity. The pilot is feasible within the $5,000 budget and team constraints, with clear success metrics and decision criteria for post-pilot evaluation.

Proceed with the baseline plan, but prepare the contingency overlay (reduced scope, ownership changes, reallocated budget) for immediate activation if a developer becomes unavailable. Prompt:
Create a 12-week strategic launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant supporting two undergraduate courses and about 300 students. The team has one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a maximum $5,000 pilot budget. The assistant may answer only from instructor-approved course materials and must include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff.

Build the baseline plan first. Then apply this required contingency at the end of Week 5: one student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8, and exactly $750 originally allocated to infrastructure must be reallocated to privacy review and testing. The total budget may not exceed $5,000. Show how scope, ownership, dependencies, testing, support, milestones, and decision gates change; do not merely shift dates.

Define measurable Expand, Revise/Extend, and Stop criteria after Week 12. Do not invent university policies, approvals, completed results, or legal guarantees. Label assumptions.

1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
This plan outlines a 12-week pilot launch of an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students). The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, and academic integrity violations, and escalates to human staff when uncertain. The team includes one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. A baseline plan is provided, followed by a contingency overlay for Weeks 6–8 where one student developer is unavailable and $750 is reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing. The total budget remains at $5,000.

Assumptions:
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting credits or existing infrastructure (e.g., virtual machine, database) at no additional cost beyond the $5,000.
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided in digital format by Week 2.
- No university policies, approvals, or legal guarantees are assumed; all safeguards are technical and procedural.
- Student developers have basic proficiency in Python, NLP libraries, and version control.
- The teaching assistant can handle up to 10 hours/week of escalation and support.
- The faculty sponsor can dedicate 2–3 hours/week for content approval and oversight.
- The pilot runs during a regular academic semester; no major holidays or exam periods conflict with Weeks 6–8.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
Goals:
- Deploy a functional AI course-QA assistant by Week 8 for two courses.
- Achieve ≥80% answer accuracy on a held-out test set of 50 instructor-approved questions by Week 10.
- Ensure <5% of student queries require human escalation by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during the pilot.
- Collect usage data (queries, accuracy, escalation rate, user satisfaction) for post-pilot evaluation.

Non-Goals:
- Not building a general-purpose AI assistant; scope is limited to two courses.
- Not replacing human teaching assistants or instructors.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or scalability.
- Not integrating with university learning management system (LMS) beyond basic API access (if available).
- Not conducting formal user research beyond pilot feedback surveys.

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
Metrics:
- Answer accuracy: % of correct answers on a held-out test set of 50 questions (target ≥80% by Week 10/11).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human staff (target <5% by Week 12).
- User satisfaction: Average rating on a 1–5 scale from optional survey (target ≥4.0).
- Privacy incidents: Number of confirmed breaches (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: Number of confirmed violations (target 0).
- Adoption rate: % of students who use the assistant at least once (target ≥50% by Week 12).

Data-Collection Plan:
- Log all queries (anonymized) with timestamps, answers, and escalation flags.
- Weekly accuracy spot-checks by TA using 10 random queries.
- Formal accuracy test at Week 10/11 with 50 pre-approved questions.
- Optional user satisfaction survey distributed at Week 10/11.
- Incident log maintained by TA and faculty sponsor.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
Governance:
- Faculty sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any new material added during pilot requires sponsor approval.
- Weekly 30-minute check-in with faculty sponsor, TA, and lead developer.

Content Approval:
- Instructor-approved materials are the sole source for answers.
- No external web search or unapproved documents allowed.
- Answer templates for common question types are pre-approved.

Escalation Process:
- If the assistant’s confidence score is below 0.7, or if the query is flagged as assignment-related, the query is escalated to the TA.
- TA responds within 4 hours during business days.
- If TA cannot answer, escalation to faculty sponsor within 24 hours.
- All escalations are logged for review.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
Expand Criteria (all must be met):
- Answer accuracy ≥85% on final test.
- Escalation rate <3%.
- User satisfaction ≥4.5.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Faculty sponsor recommends expansion to additional courses.

Revise/Extend Criteria (any one met):
- Accuracy between 70% and 84%.
- Escalation rate between 3% and 8%.
- User satisfaction between 3.5 and 4.4.
- Minor privacy or integrity incident (resolved without data exposure).
- Faculty sponsor requests additional features or extended pilot.

Stop Criteria (any one met):
- Accuracy <70%.
- Escalation rate >8%.
- User satisfaction <3.5.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget exceeded or unsustainable.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan as outlined, with the contingency overlay prepared for Weeks 6–8. The reallocation of $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing is a prudent risk mitigation that does not compromise core functionality, as existing university credits can cover reduced infrastructure needs. The delayed soft launch for Course B (Week 9) and shifted decision gates ensure quality is maintained despite reduced developer capacity. The pilot is feasible within the $5,000 budget and team constraints, with clear success metrics and decision criteria for post-pilot evaluation.

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
Metrics:

- Escalation rate: Percentage of queries escalated to human.
- Hallucination rate: Percentage of answers with incorrect information (sampled).
- Privacy incidents: Number of PII leaks (target: 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: Number of attempts to get graded answers (target: 0).
- User satisfaction: Survey score (1-5) at Week 10.
- Budget adherence: Total spend vs. $5,000.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
Governance:
- Faculty Sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any changes to the assistant’s knowledge base require faculty approval.
- Weekly team check-ins (15 minutes) to review progress and issues.

Content approval:
- Only instructor-approved materials (syllabus, lecture notes, readings) are ingested.
- Faculty Sponsor reviews and signs off on the final content list by Week 2.
- No external sources (e.g., web search) are allowed.

Escalation process:
- If the assistant cannot answer with high confidence (below threshold), it responds: “I cannot answer this with confidence. Please contact your TA or instructor.”
- If a query appears to be a graded assignment, the assistant responds: “I cannot help with graded assignments. Please contact your instructor.”
- All escalations are logged and forwarded to the Teaching Assistant, who responds within 24 hours.
- If the TA cannot resolve, the Faculty Sponsor is notified.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
Expand criteria (all must be met):
- Usage: At least 50% of students (150 out of 300) have used the assistant at least once.
- Accuracy: At least 90% of sampled queries are answered correctly.
- Escalation rate: Less than 10% of queries are escalated.
- Hallucination rate: Less than 2% of sampled answers contain incorrect information.
- Privacy incidents: Zero.
- Academic integrity incidents: Zero.
- User satisfaction: Average survey score of 4.0 or higher (out of 5).
- Budget: Under $5,000.
- Decision: Propose expansion to additional courses or full deployment.

Revise/Extend criteria (if some metrics are met but not all):
- Usage: At least 30% of students have used the assistant.
- Accuracy: At least 80% of sampled queries are answered correctly.
- Escalation rate: Less than 20%.
- Hallucination rate: Less than 5%.
- Privacy and integrity incidents: Zero.
- User satisfaction: Average survey score of 3.0 or higher.
- Budget: Under $5,000.
- Decision: Revise the assistant (e.g., improve content, safeguards, or user interface) and extend the pilot for another 4-8 weeks.

Stop criteria (if any of the following occur):
- Privacy breach (any PII leak).
- Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant provides answers to graded assignments).
- Hallucination rate above 10% after mitigation attempts.
- Budget overrun beyond $5,000.
- User satisfaction below 2.0.
- Decision: Shut down the pilot and do not deploy further.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan as described, with the contingency plan ready for implementation at the end of Week 5 if Student Developer B becomes unavailable. The contingency reduces scope to one course during Weeks 6-8 but maintains core functionality and expands privacy review and testing. The budget remains within $5,000. The pilot is feasible given the team size and resources, and the decision criteria provide clear guidance for future steps.

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
Metrics:
- Usage: Number of queries per week, unique users, topics.
- Accuracy: Percentage of queries answered correctly (sampled by TA).
- Escalation rate: Percentage of queries escalated to human.
- Hallucination rate: Percentage of answers with incorrect information (sampled).
- Privacy incidents: Number of PII leaks (target: 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: Number of attempts to get graded answers (target: 0).
- User satisfaction: Survey score (1-5) at Week 10.
- Budget adherence: Total spend vs. $5,000.

Data-collection plan:
- Log all queries (anonymized) with timestamps, topics, and responses.
- TA manually reviews a random 10% of queries weekly for accuracy and hallucination.
- Track escalation events and reasons.
- Survey students in Week 10 (optional, anonymous).
- Monitor API usage and costs weekly.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
Governance:
- Faculty Sponsor approves all course materials before ingestion.
- Any changes to the assistant’s knowledge base require faculty approval.
- Weekly team check-ins (15 minutes) to review progress and issues.

Content approval:
- Only instructor-approved materials (syllabus, lecture notes, readings) are ingested.
- Faculty Sponsor reviews and signs off on the final content list by Week 2.
- No external sources (e.g., web search) are allowed.

Escalation process:
- If the assistant cannot answer with high confidence (below threshold), it responds: “I cannot answer this with confidence. Please contact your TA or instructor.”
- If a query appears to be a graded assignment, the assistant responds: “I cannot help with graded assignments. Please contact your instructor.”
- All escalations are logged and forwarded to the Teaching Assistant, who responds within 24 hours.
- If the TA cannot resolve, the Faculty Sponsor is notified.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
Expand criteria (all must be met):
- Usage: At least 50% of students (150 out of 300) have used the assistant at least once.
- Accuracy: At least 90% of sampled queries are answered correctly.
- Escalation rate: Less than 10% of queries are escalated.
- Hallucination rate: Less than 2% of sampled answers contain incorrect information.
- Privacy incidents: Zero.
- Academic integrity incidents: Zero.
- User satisfaction: Average survey score of 4.0 or higher (out of 5).
- Budget: Under $5,000.
- Decision: Propose expansion to additional courses or full deployment.

Revise/Extend criteria (if some metrics are met but not all):
- Usage: At least 30% of students have used the assistant.
- Accuracy: At least 80% of sampled queries are answered correctly.
- Escalation rate: Less than 20%.
- Hallucination rate: Less than 5%.
- Privacy and integrity incidents: Zero.
- User satisfaction: Average survey score of 3.0 or higher.
- Budget: Under $5,000.
- Decision: Revise the assistant (e.g., improve content, safeguards, or user interface) and extend the pilot for another 4-8 weeks.

Stop criteria (if any of the following occur):
- Privacy breach (any PII leak).
- Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant provides answers to graded assignments).
- Hallucination rate above 10% after mitigation attempts.
- Budget overrun beyond $5,000.
- User satisfaction below 2.0.
- Decision: Shut down the pilot and do not deploy further.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan as described, with the contingency plan ready for implementation at the end of Week 5 if Student Developer B becomes unavailable. The contingency reduces scope to one course during Weeks 6-8 but maintains core functionality and expands privacy review and testing. The budget remains within $5,000. The pilot is feasible given the team size and resources, and the decision criteria provide clear guidance for future steps.

agent_effective_config.description: Low-latency agent and optional Judge profile.

agent_task.prompt:
Create a 12-week strategic launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant supporting two undergraduate courses and about 300 students. The team has one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a maximum $5,000 pilot budget. The assistant may answer only from instructor-approved course materials and must include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff.

Build the baseline plan first. Then apply this required contingency at the end of Week 5: one student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8, and exactly $750 originally allocated to infrastructure must be reallocated to privacy review and testing. The total budget may not exceed $5,000. Show how scope, ownership, dependencies, testing, support, milestones, and decision gates change; do not merely shift dates.

Define measurable Expand, Revise/Extend, and Stop criteria after Week 12. Do not invent university policies, approvals, completed results, or legal guarantees. Label assumptions.
agent_task.required_output_format:
Use the following structure:
1. Executive summary and assumptions
2. Goals and non-goals
3. Stakeholder and decision-rights map
4. Baseline 12-week roadmap table
5. Baseline resource and budget allocation
6. Week 6-8 contingency overlay and ownership changes
7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation
8. Risk register and mitigations
9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
12. Final recommendation
